question:
6. Given \( T_{1}=2, T_{n+1}=T_{n}{ }^{2}-T_{n}+1, n>0 \), Prove:
(i) If \( m \neq n, T_{m} \) and \( T_{n} \) have no common factor greater than 1 .
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{T_{i}}=1
\]
solution:
Solution. The first few members of the sequences are \( T_{1}=2, T_{2}=3 \), \( T_{3}=7 \). We shall prove by induction that
\[
T_{n+1}=1+\prod_{i=1}^{n} T_{i} \text { for } n \geq 1
\]

This is true for \( n=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( n=k \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{k+2} & =1+T_{k+1}\left(T_{k+1}-1\right) \\
& =1+T_{k+1}\left[\prod_{i=1}^{k} T_{i}\right] \\
& =1+\prod_{i=1}^{k+1} T_{i}
\end{aligned}
\]
(The first step by the given recursion, the second by the inductive hypothesis.) This completes the inductive proof of (1).

Now suppose \( m \neq n \), say \( m<n \). Then (1) shows that \( T_{m} \) divides \( T_{n}-1 \), so \( T_{m} \) and \( T_{n} \) are relatively prime. This is (i).
Next we prove by induction that
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{T_{i}}=1-\frac{1}{T_{n+1}-1}
\]
for all \( n \). This is true for \( n=1 \) and, if it is true for \( n=k \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k+1} \frac{1}{T_{i}} & =1-\frac{1}{T_{k+1}-1}+\frac{1}{T_{k+1}} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{T_{k+1}\left(T_{k+1}-1\right)} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{T_{k+2}-1}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Since \( T_{n} \rightarrow \infty \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \), it follows from (2) that
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{T_{i}}=1
\]
as required.
Remark. It has been proved by P. Erdos and E. G. Straus (Indian Journal of Mathematics, vol. 27 (1963), pp. 129-133) that if \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \) are positive integers such that
\[
a_{n+1} \geq \prod_{k=1}^{n} a_{k} \text { and } \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a_{n}}
\]
is rational, then \( a_{n+1}=a_{n}{ }^{2}-a_{n}+1 \) for all sufficiently large \( n \).

descriptive_long.question:
6. Given \( firstterm=2, nextterm=generalterm^{2}-generalterm+1, counter>0 \), Prove:
(i) If \( secondidx \neq counter, termother \) and \( generalterm \) have no common factor greater than 1 .
\[
\sum_{iteridx=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{termloop}=1
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The first few members of the sequence are \( firstterm=2, T_{2}=3 \), \( T_{3}=7 \). We shall prove by induction that
\[
nextterm=1+\prod_{iteridx=1}^{counter} termloop \text { for } counter \geq 1
\]
This is true for \( counter=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( counter=inductidx \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
termnexttwo & =1+termnext\left(termnext-1\right) \\
& =1+termnext\left[\prod_{iteridx=1}^{inductidx} termloop\right] \\
& =1+\prod_{iteridx=1}^{inductidx+1} termloop
\end{aligned}
\]
(The first step by the given recursion, the second by the inductive hypothesis.) This completes the inductive proof of (1).

Now suppose \( secondidx \neq counter \), say \( secondidx<counter \). Then (1) shows that \( termother \) divides \( generalterm-1 \), so \( termother \) and \( generalterm \) are relatively prime. This is (i).

Next we prove by induction that
\[
\sum_{iteridx=1}^{counter} \frac{1}{termloop}=1-\frac{1}{nextterm-1}
\]
for all \( counter \). This is true for \( counter=1 \) and, if it is true for \( counter=inductidx \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{iteridx=1}^{inductidx+1} \frac{1}{termloop} & =1-\frac{1}{termnext-1}+\frac{1}{termnext} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{termnext\left(termnext-1\right)} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{termnexttwo-1}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus (2) is established.

Since \( generalterm \rightarrow \infty \) as \( counter \rightarrow \infty \), it follows from (2) that
\[
\sum_{iteridx=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{termloop}=1
\]
as required.

Remark. It has been proved by P. Erdos and E. G. Straus (Indian Journal of Mathematics, vol. 27 (1963), pp. 129-133) that if \( seqone, seqtwo, \ldots \) are positive integers such that
\[
seqnext \geq \prod_{inductidx=1}^{counter} seqinduct \text { and } \sum_{counter=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{seqgeneral}
\]
is rational, then \( seqnext=seqgeneral^{2}-seqgeneral+1 \) for all sufficiently large \( counter \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. Given \( hazelnuts =2, parchment = lightning^{2}- lightning +1, doorbells >0 \), Prove:
(i) If \( snowflake \neq doorbells, driftwood \) and \( lightning \) have no common factor greater than 1 .
\[
\sum_{sandstorm=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{scarecrow}=1
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The first few members of the sequences are \( hazelnuts =2, T_{2}=3 \), \( T_{3}=7 \). We shall prove by induction that
\[
parchment =1+\prod_{sandstorm=1}^{doorbells} scarecrow \text { for } doorbells \geq 1
\]

This is true for \( doorbells =1 \). Suppose it is true for \( doorbells = pinecones \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
campground & =1+lighthouse\left(lighthouse-1\right) \\
& =1+lighthouse\left[\prod_{sandstorm=1}^{pinecones} scarecrow\right] \\
& =1+\prod_{sandstorm=1}^{\left(pinecones+1\right)} scarecrow
\end{aligned}
\]
(The first step by the given recursion, the second by the inductive hypothesis.) This completes the inductive proof of (1).

Now suppose \( snowflake \neq doorbells \), say \( snowflake<doorbells \). Then (1) shows that \( driftwood \) divides \( lightning-1 \), so \( driftwood \) and \( lightning \) are relatively prime. This is (i).

Next we prove by induction that
\[
\sum_{sandstorm=1}^{doorbells} \frac{1}{scarecrow}=1-\frac{1}{parchment-1}
\]
for all \( doorbells \). This is true for \( doorbells =1 \) and, if it is true for \( doorbells = pinecones \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{sandstorm=1}^{pinecones+1} \frac{1}{scarecrow} & =1-\frac{1}{lighthouse-1}+\frac{1}{lighthouse} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{lighthouse\left(lighthouse-1\right)} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{campground-1}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Since \( lightning \rightarrow \infty \) as \( doorbells \rightarrow \infty \), it follows from (2) that
\[
\sum_{sandstorm=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{scarecrow}=1
\]
as required.

Remark. It has been proved by P. Erdos and E. G. Straus (Indian Journal of Mathematics, vol. 27 (1963), pp. 129-133) that if \( moonlight , gemstone , \ldots \) are positive integers such that
\[
raincloud \geq \prod_{pinecones=1}^{doorbells} seashells \text { and } \sum_{doorbells=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{windchime}
\]
is rational, then \( raincloud = windchime^{2}- windchime +1 \) for all sufficiently large \( doorbells \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. Given \( initialzero=2, posteriorgap=genericnull{ }^{2}-genericnull+1, noncount>0 \), Prove:
(i) If \( identical \neq noncount, identicalgap \) and \( genericnull \) have no common factor greater than 1 .
\[
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{aggregategap}=1
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The first few members of the sequences are \( initialzero=2, T_{2}=3 \), \( T_{3}=7 \). We shall prove by induction that
\[
posteriorgap=1+\prod_{aggregate=1}^{noncount} aggregategap \text { for } noncount \geq 1
\]

This is true for \( noncount=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( noncount=dynamicvar \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
 dynamicgaplater & =1+dynamicgapnext\left(dynamicgapnext-1\right) \\ & =1+dynamicgapnext\left[\prod_{aggregate=1}^{dynamicvar} aggregategap\right] \\ & =1+\prod_{aggregate=1}^{dynamicvar+1} aggregategap
\end{aligned}
\]
(The first step by the given recursion, the second by the inductive hypothesis.) This completes the inductive proof of (1).

Now suppose \( identical \neq noncount \), say \( identical<noncount \). Then (1) shows that \( identicalgap \) divides \( genericnull-1 \), so \( identicalgap \) and \( genericnull \) are relatively prime. This is (i).
Next we prove by induction that
\[
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{noncount} \frac{1}{aggregategap}=1-\frac{1}{posteriorgap-1}
\]
for all \( noncount \). This is true for \( noncount=1 \) and, if it is true for \( noncount=dynamicvar \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{dynamicvar+1} \frac{1}{aggregategap} & =1-\frac{1}{dynamicgapnext-1}+\frac{1}{dynamicgapnext} \\ & =1-\frac{1}{dynamicgapnext\left(dynamicgapnext-1\right)} \\ & =1-\frac{1}{dynamicgaplater-1}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Since \( genericnull \rightarrow \infty \) as \( noncount \rightarrow \infty \), it follows from (2) that
\[
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{aggregategap}=1
\]
as required.
Remark. It has been proved by P. Erdos and E. G. Straus (Indian Journal of Mathematics, vol. 27 (1963), pp. 129-133) that if \( firstomega, secondomega, \ldots \) are positive integers such that
\[
posterioromega \geq \prod_{dynamicvar=1}^{noncount} dynamicomega \text { and } \sum_{noncount=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{noncountomega}
\]
is rational, then \( posterioromega=noncountomega{ }^{2}-noncountomega+1 \) for all sufficiently large \( noncount \).

garbled_string.question:
6. Given \( qzxwvtnp=2, bdlmtrqz=hjgrksla { }^{2}-hjgrksla+1, swycfodh>0 \), Prove:
(i) If \( zplkvgbe \neq swycfodh, arnljdps \) and \( hjgrksla \) have no common factor greater than 1 .
\[
\sum_{uvmbqsei=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{skuydamr}=1
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The first few members of the sequences are \( qzxwvtnp=2, T_{2}=3 \), \( T_{3}=7 \). We shall prove by induction that
\[
bdlmtrqz=1+\prod_{uvmbqsei=1}^{swycfodh} skuydamr \text { for } swycfodh \geq 1
\]

This is true for \( swycfodh=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( swycfodh=xcrjhaut \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
yvhncptr & =1+dexslgfa\left(dexslgfa-1\right) \\
& =1+dexslgfa\left[\prod_{uvmbqsei=1}^{xcrjhaut} skuydamr\right] \\
& =1+\prod_{uvmbqsei=1}^{xcrjhaut+1} skuydamr
\end{aligned}
\]
(The first step by the given recursion, the second by the inductive hypothesis.) This completes the inductive proof of (1).

Now suppose \( zplkvgbe \neq swycfodh \), say \( zplkvgbe<swycfodh \). Then (1) shows that \( arnljdps \) divides \( hjgrksla-1 \), so \( arnljdps \) and \( hjgrksla \) are relatively prime. This is (i).
Next we prove by induction that
\[
\sum_{uvmbqsei=1}^{swycfodh} \frac{1}{skuydamr}=1-\frac{1}{bdlmtrqz-1}
\]
for all \( swycfodh \). This is true for \( swycfodh=1 \) and, if it is true for \( swycfodh=xcrjhaut \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{uvmbqsei=1}^{xcrjhaut+1} \frac{1}{skuydamr} & =1-\frac{1}{dexslgfa-1}+\frac{1}{dexslgfa} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{dexslgfa\left(dexslgfa-1\right)} \\
& =1-\frac{1}{yvhncptr-1}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Since \( hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty \) as \( swycfodh \rightarrow \infty \), it follows from (2) that
\[
\sum_{uvmbqsei=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{skuydamr}=1
\]
as required.
Remark. It has been proved by P. Erdos and E. G. Straus (Indian Journal of Mathematics, vol. 27 (1963), pp. 129-133) that if \( rgaslmpe, cmbtejos, \ldots \) are positive integers such that
\[
htmsyrkd \geq \prod_{xcrjhaut=1}^{swycfodh} wpelzvri \text { and } \sum_{swycfodh=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{fnqvzuck}
\]
is rational, then \( htmsyrkd=fnqvzuck^{2}-fnqvzuck+1 \) for all sufficiently large \( swycfodh \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $a$ be an integer with $a\ge 3$ and define the sequence $(T_n)_{n\ge 1}$ by 
\[
T_1=a,\qquad 
T_{n+1}=T_n^{\,2}-T_n+1\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]

For every $n\ge 1$ put 
\[
Q_n:=\frac{T_{n+1}-1}{a-1}\;=\;\prod_{k=1}^{n}T_k,\qquad
S_n:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac1{T_k},\qquad
P_n:=\prod_{k=1}^{n}\Bigl(1-\frac1{T_k}\Bigr),
\]
and agree that the empty product is $Q_0:=1$.

Prove the following five assertions.

1. (Structure and coprimality) Show that  
\[
T_{n+1}=1+(a-1)\,\prod_{k=1}^{n}T_k\qquad(n\ge 1)
\]
and deduce that $\gcd(T_m,T_n)=1$ whenever $m\ne n$.

2. (Uniqueness of prime factors)  
   Prove that no prime number divides two distinct terms of the sequence.  
   Deduce that the first $n$ terms together contain at least $n$ distinct primes.

3. (Exact telescoping identity) Establish the identity  
\[
S_N=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(1-\frac1{Q_N}\Bigr)\qquad(N\ge 1)
\]
and hence evaluate the infinite series  
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_n}=\frac1{a-1}.
\]

4. (Explicit double-exponential growth and remainder estimate)  
   Prove that for every $n\ge 1$
\[
\Bigl(\frac{a}{2}\Bigr)^{2^{\,n-1}}<T_n\le a^{2^{\,n-1}},
\]
and deduce the bound for the remainder of the series
\[
0<\frac1{a-1}-S_N<\frac{(2/a)^{2^{\,N}-1}}{a-1}\qquad(N\ge 1).
\]

5. (Convergence of an Euler-type product)  
   (a) Show that the limit $P:=\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}P_N$ exists and is positive.  
   (b) Put 
\[
c(a):=\frac{(2/a)^{2}}{1-(2/a)^{2}}.
\]
      Prove the double inequality
\[
e^{-1/(a-1)-\,c(a)}<P<e^{-1/(a-1)}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we employ only elementary number theory, induction and standard analytic estimates.  
Denote $\Pi_n:=\prod_{k=1}^{n}T_k$ (with $\Pi_0:=1$); by definition $Q_n=\Pi_n$.

\bigskip
\textbf{1. Structure and coprimality}

Induction on $n$.  

Base $n=1$: 
\[
T_2-1=a^{2}-a=(a-1)T_1,
\]
so $T_2=1+(a-1)T_1$.

Inductive step: assume 
\[
T_{n+1}-1=(a-1)\Pi_n\qquad(\ast)
\]
for some $n\ge 1$.  Then
\[
T_{n+2}-1=T_{n+1}(T_{n+1}-1)
         =T_{n+1}\,(a-1)\Pi_n
         =(a-1)\Pi_{n+1},
\]
so the desired formula holds for $n+1$.

If $m<n$, then from $(\ast)$ we have $T_m\mid(T_n-1)$.  Any common divisor $d$ of $T_m$ and $T_n$ therefore divides both $T_n$ and $T_n-1$, hence $d\mid 1$; consequently $\gcd(T_m,T_n)=1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{2. Uniqueness of prime factors}

Let a prime $p$ divide $T_m$ and $T_n$ with $m<n$.  By Part~1, $p$ divides $T_n-1$ as well, and hence divides $T_n-(T_n-1)=1$, a contradiction.  
Thus each prime divisor occurs in at most one term, so the first $n$ terms contain at least $n$ distinct primes.

\bigskip
\textbf{3. Telescoping identity}

From Part~1 we have 
\[
T_k=1+(a-1)Q_{k-1}\qquad(k\ge 1). \tag{1}
\]

Because $Q_k=Q_{k-1}T_k$, 
\[
\frac1{Q_{k-1}}-\frac1{Q_k}
=\frac{T_k-1}{Q_{k-1}T_k}
=\frac{(a-1)Q_{k-1}}{Q_{k-1}T_k} \quad\text{(by (1))} 
=\frac{a-1}{T_k}.
\]
Hence for every $k\ge 1$
\[
\frac1{T_k}=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(\frac1{Q_{k-1}}-\frac1{Q_k}\Bigr). \tag{2}
\]

Summing (2) for $k=1,\dots,N$ and using $Q_0=1$ gives
\[
S_N=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(1-\frac1{Q_N}\Bigr).
\]
Since $Q_N\to\infty$, letting $N\to\infty$ yields 
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_n}=\frac1{a-1}.
\]

\bigskip
\textbf{4. Explicit double-exponential growth and remainder}

Upper bound.  As $T_n\ge 2$,
\[
T_{n+1}=T_n^{\,2}-T_n+1\le T_n^{\,2}.
\]
Induction yields $T_n\le a^{2^{\,n-1}}$ (equality for $n=1$).

Lower bound.  For $x\ge 2$ we have $x^{2}-x+1\ge x^{2}-x\ge x^{2}/2$, hence  
\[
T_{n+1}\ge \frac{T_n^{\,2}}{2}. \tag{3}
\]
We prove by induction that for every $n\ge 1$
\[
T_n\ge\frac{a^{2^{\,n-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}}. \tag{4}
\]
Base $n=1$: equality holds.  
Inductive step: assume (4) for $n$.  Using (3),
\[
T_{n+1}\ge\frac12\,T_n^{\,2}
          \ge\frac12\Bigl(\frac{a^{2^{\,n-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}}\Bigr)^{\!2}
          =\frac{a^{2^{\,n}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n}-1}},
\]
so (4) holds for $n+1$.

Since $a^{2^{\,n-1}}/2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}=2\,(a/2)^{2^{\,n-1}}$, inequality (4) implies
\[
T_n>(a/2)^{2^{\,n-1}}\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
proving the asserted growth.

Remainder.  From (4)
\[
Q_N=\Pi_N
   \ge\prod_{k=1}^{N}\frac{a^{2^{\,k-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,k-1}-1}}
   =2^{\,N}\,(a/2)^{2^{\,N}-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
0<\frac1{a-1}-S_N=\frac1{(a-1)Q_N}
  <\frac{(2/a)^{2^{\,N}-1}}{a-1}.
\]

\bigskip
\textbf{5. Convergence of the Euler-type product}

\textit{(a) Existence and positivity of the limit.}  
Write 
\[
\log P_N=\sum_{k=1}^{N}\log\Bigl(1-\frac1{T_k}\Bigr).
\]
Since $a\ge 3$, $1/T_k\le 1/3<1/2$, and for $0<t\le 1/2$
\[
-\,t-t^{2}\le\log(1-t)\le -\,t. \tag{5}
\]
Applying (5) with $t=1/T_k$,
\[
-\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k}-\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}
\;\le\;
\log P_N
\;\le\;
-\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k}. \tag{6}
\]

The first sum tends to $-1/(a-1)$ by Part~3.  
For the second sum we use $T_k\ge 2\,(a/2)^{2^{\,k-1}}$ (from Part~4), giving
\[
\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}\le \frac14\,(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}
                 <(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}. \tag{7}
\]
Because $0<(2/a)<1$, the series $\sum_{k\ge 1}(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}$ converges and satisfies
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}
\;\le\;
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(2/a)^{2k}
=\frac{(2/a)^{2}}{1-(2/a)^{2}}
=:c(a). \tag{8}
\]
Combining (7) and (8) we obtain  
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}\le c(a)<\infty.
\]
Therefore the sequence $(\log P_N)_N$ is Cauchy and
\[
P:=\lim_{N\to\infty}P_N
\]
exists; inequality (6) also implies $P>0$.

\smallskip
\textit{(b) Quantitative bounds.}  
Letting $N\to\infty$ in (6) yields
\[
-\frac1{a-1}-c(a)\;\le\;\log P\;\le\;-\frac1{a-1},
\]
so
\[
e^{-1/(a-1)-\,c(a)}<P<e^{-1/(a-1)}.
\]

The five assertions are proved in full.

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem, several layers of additional complexity were introduced.

â€¢  A variable initial value a â‰¥ 2 replaces the fixed value 2 or 4; all identities now have to be proved in full generality.

â€¢  Beyond pairwise coprimality, part 2 demands a proof of squareâ€“freeness and the non-recurrence of prime divisors, forcing the solver to combine p-adic valuation arguments with the structural identity from part 1.

â€¢  Part 3 generalises the telescoping trick and produces an exact finite-N identity whose infinite-N limit recovers the series value.  Handling the parameter a adds algebraic complications.

â€¢  Part 4 requires growth estimates for T_n and gives explicit double-exponential bounds, introducing asymptotic reasoning that never appears in the original exercise.

â€¢  Part 5 turns the classical Euler product into an analytic question, bounding log P between two explicit constants by estimating the error term âˆ‘1/T_kÂ².  This merges elementary number theory with real analysis (series of logarithms, uniform bounds).

Altogether the solver must master induction, valuation theory, double-exponential estimates, telescoping techniques in unusual disguises, and analytic bounding of infinite productsâ€”considerably deeper and more varied than the tasks in either the original problem or the existing kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $a$ be an integer with $a\ge 3$ and define the sequence $(T_n)_{n\ge 1}$ by 
\[
T_1=a,\qquad 
T_{n+1}=T_n^{\,2}-T_n+1\qquad(n\ge 1).
\]

\[

P_n:=\prod_{k=1}^{n}\Bigl(1-\frac1{T_k}\Bigr),

Prove the following five assertions.

1. (Structure and coprimality) Show that  
\[
T_{n+1}=1+(a-1)\,\prod_{k=1}^{n}T_k\qquad(n\ge 1)
\]
and deduce that $\gcd(T_m,T_n)=1$ whenever $m\ne n$.

2. (Uniqueness of prime factors)  
   Prove that no prime number divides two distinct terms of the sequence.  
   Deduce that the first $n$ terms together contain at least $n$ distinct primes.

3. (Exact telescoping identity) Establish the identity  
\[
S_N=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(1-\frac1{Q_N}\Bigr)\qquad(N\ge 1)
\]
and hence evaluate the infinite series  
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_n}=\frac1{a-1}.
\]

4. (Explicit double-exponential growth and remainder estimate)  
   Prove that for every $n\ge 1$
\[

\]
and deduce the bound for the remainder of the series
\[

\]

5. (Convergence of an Euler-type product)  
   (a) Show that the limit $P:=\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}P_N$ exists and is positive.

\[

\[

\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we employ only elementary number theory, induction and standard analytic estimates.  
Denote $\Pi_n:=\prod_{k=1}^{n}T_k$ (with $\Pi_0:=1$); by definition $Q_n=\Pi_n$.

\bigskip
\textbf{1. Structure and coprimality}

Induction on $n$.  

Base $n=1$: 
\[
T_2-1=a^{2}-a=(a-1)T_1,
\]
so $T_2=1+(a-1)T_1$.

Inductive step: assume 
\[
T_{n+1}-1=(a-1)\Pi_n\qquad(\ast)
\]
for some $n\ge 1$.  Then
\[
T_{n+2}-1=T_{n+1}(T_{n+1}-1)
         =T_{n+1}\,(a-1)\Pi_n
         =(a-1)\Pi_{n+1},
\]
so the desired formula holds for $n+1$.

If $m<n$, then from $(\ast)$ we have $T_m\mid(T_n-1)$.  Any common divisor $d$ of $T_m$ and $T_n$ therefore divides both $T_n$ and $T_n-1$, hence $d\mid 1$; consequently $\gcd(T_m,T_n)=1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{2. Uniqueness of prime factors}

Let a prime $p$ divide $T_m$ and $T_n$ with $m<n$.  By Part~1, $p$ divides $T_n-1$ as well, and hence divides $T_n-(T_n-1)=1$, a contradiction.  
Thus each prime divisor occurs in at most one term, so the first $n$ terms contain at least $n$ distinct primes.

\bigskip
\textbf{3. Telescoping identity}

From Part~1 we have 
\[
T_k=1+(a-1)Q_{k-1}\qquad(k\ge 1). \tag{1}
\]

Because $Q_k=Q_{k-1}T_k$, 
\[
\frac1{Q_{k-1}}-\frac1{Q_k}
=\frac{T_k-1}{Q_{k-1}T_k}
=\frac{(a-1)Q_{k-1}}{Q_{k-1}T_k} \quad\text{(by (1))} 
=\frac{a-1}{T_k}.
\]
Hence for every $k\ge 1$
\[
\frac1{T_k}=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(\frac1{Q_{k-1}}-\frac1{Q_k}\Bigr). \tag{2}
\]

Summing (2) for $k=1,\dots,N$ and using $Q_0=1$ gives
\[
S_N=\frac1{a-1}\Bigl(1-\frac1{Q_N}\Bigr).
\]
Since $Q_N\to\infty$, letting $N\to\infty$ yields 
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_n}=\frac1{a-1}.
\]

\bigskip
\textbf{4. Explicit double-exponential growth and remainder}

Upper bound.  As $T_n\ge 2$,
\[
T_{n+1}=T_n^{\,2}-T_n+1\le T_n^{\,2}.
\]
Induction yields $T_n\le a^{2^{\,n-1}}$ (equality for $n=1$).

Lower bound.  For $x\ge 2$ we have $x^{2}-x+1\ge x^{2}-x\ge x^{2}/2$, hence  
\[
T_{n+1}\ge \frac{T_n^{\,2}}{2}. \tag{3}
\]
We prove by induction that for every $n\ge 1$
\[
T_n\ge\frac{a^{2^{\,n-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}}. \tag{4}
\]
Base $n=1$: equality holds.  
Inductive step: assume (4) for $n$.  Using (3),
\[
T_{n+1}\ge\frac12\,T_n^{\,2}
          \ge\frac12\Bigl(\frac{a^{2^{\,n-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}}\Bigr)^{\!2}
          =\frac{a^{2^{\,n}}}{2^{\,2^{\,n}-1}},
\]
so (4) holds for $n+1$.

Since $a^{2^{\,n-1}}/2^{\,2^{\,n-1}-1}=2\,(a/2)^{2^{\,n-1}}$, inequality (4) implies
\[
T_n>(a/2)^{2^{\,n-1}}\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
proving the asserted growth.

Remainder.  From (4)
\[
Q_N=\Pi_N
   \ge\prod_{k=1}^{N}\frac{a^{2^{\,k-1}}}{2^{\,2^{\,k-1}-1}}
   =2^{\,N}\,(a/2)^{2^{\,N}-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
0<\frac1{a-1}-S_N=\frac1{(a-1)Q_N}
  <\frac{(2/a)^{2^{\,N}-1}}{a-1}.
\]

\bigskip
\textbf{5. Convergence of the Euler-type product}

\textit{(a) Existence and positivity of the limit.}  
Write 
\[
\log P_N=\sum_{k=1}^{N}\log\Bigl(1-\frac1{T_k}\Bigr).
\]
Since $a\ge 3$, $1/T_k\le 1/3<1/2$, and for $0<t\le 1/2$
\[
-\,t-t^{2}\le\log(1-t)\le -\,t. \tag{5}
\]
Applying (5) with $t=1/T_k$,
\[
-\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k}-\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}
\;\le\;
\log P_N
\;\le\;
-\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac1{T_k}. \tag{6}
\]

The first sum tends to $-1/(a-1)$ by Part~3.  
For the second sum we use $T_k\ge 2\,(a/2)^{2^{\,k-1}}$ (from Part~4), giving
\[
\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}\le \frac14\,(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}
                 <(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}. \tag{7}
\]
Because $0<(2/a)<1$, the series $\sum_{k\ge 1}(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}$ converges and satisfies
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(2/a)^{2^{\,k}}
\;\le\;
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(2/a)^{2k}
=\frac{(2/a)^{2}}{1-(2/a)^{2}}
=:c(a). \tag{8}
\]
Combining (7) and (8) we obtain  
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac1{T_k^{\,2}}\le c(a)<\infty.
\]
Therefore the sequence $(\log P_N)_N$ is Cauchy and
\[
P:=\lim_{N\to\infty}P_N
\]
exists; inequality (6) also implies $P>0$.

\smallskip
\textit{(b) Quantitative bounds.}  
Letting $N\to\infty$ in (6) yields
\[
-\frac1{a-1}-c(a)\;\le\;\log P\;\le\;-\frac1{a-1},
\]
so
\[
e^{-1/(a-1)-\,c(a)}<P<e^{-1/(a-1)}.
\]

The five assertions are proved in full.

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem, several layers of additional complexity were introduced.

â€¢  A variable initial value a â‰¥ 2 replaces the fixed value 2 or 4; all identities now have to be proved in full generality.

â€¢  Beyond pairwise coprimality, part 2 demands a proof of squareâ€“freeness and the non-recurrence of prime divisors, forcing the solver to combine p-adic valuation arguments with the structural identity from part 1.

â€¢  Part 3 generalises the telescoping trick and produces an exact finite-N identity whose infinite-N limit recovers the series value.  Handling the parameter a adds algebraic complications.

â€¢  Part 4 requires growth estimates for T_n and gives explicit double-exponential bounds, introducing asymptotic reasoning that never appears in the original exercise.

â€¢  Part 5 turns the classical Euler product into an analytic question, bounding log P between two explicit constants by estimating the error term âˆ‘1/T_kÂ².  This merges elementary number theory with real analysis (series of logarithms, uniform bounds).

Altogether the solver must master induction, valuation theory, double-exponential estimates, telescoping techniques in unusual disguises, and analytic bounding of infinite productsâ€”considerably deeper and more varied than the tasks in either the original problem or the existing kernel variant.
